summary:
Dholavira, located on Khadir Island in the Rann of Kutch in the present-day Indian state of Gujarat, was one of the largest and most elaborately planned cities of the [[event:46|Indus Valley Civilisation]], occupied from approximately 2650 to 2100 BC. The site is notable for its remarkably sophisticated water management system, its well-preserved city plan, and a series of large reservoirs and drainage channels that allowed the city to thrive in a semi-arid environment with highly seasonal rainfall.

The city was divided into at least three zones — a citadel, a middle town, and a lower town — separated by massive stone walls. Its water conservation infrastructure is extraordinary: the inhabitants constructed at least sixteen reservoirs of varying sizes, fed by carefully engineered channels and dams that captured and stored monsoon rainfall and river runoff. This hydraulic engineering represents one of the most sophisticated water management systems in the ancient world and testifies to the planning capacity and civic organisation of Indus Valley urban societies.

Dholavira has yielded significant archaeological finds, including the famous Dholavira signboard — a large wooden board with ten characters of the still-undeciphered Indus script, possibly displayed as a public inscription at a city gateway. The site's unique monumental inscriptions, its water systems, and the quality of its stone architecture distinguish it from other Indus cities. Dholavira was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2021, recognising its outstanding universal value as evidence of the urban sophistication of the Indus Valley Civilisation.
